Identifying heterogeneity in the effects of interventions across individuals is a central problem in various fields.
Developing machine learning models to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects using observational or experimental data.
Most of these models only provide point estimates of the conditional average treatment effect (CATE).
Uncertainty in these estimates arises from both errors in the model and the variation of individual treatment effects (ITEs) for individuals with the same covariates.
We adopt a predictive inference approach to this problem, with the goal of devising valid procedures to issue predictive intervals that cover ITEs on unseen data with a predetermined probability.
Traditionally, predictive inference on ITEs has been conducted through Bayesian methods.
ITEs are the focus of predictive inference methods like BART and Gaussian processes.
Methods like BART and Gaussian processes can provide interval-valued predictions of ITEs through their posterior distributions.
Bayesian methods have traditionally been used for predictive inference on ITEs.
Bayesian methods like BART have been used for predictive inference on ITEs.
Bayesian methods like Gaussian processes have been used for predictive inference on ITEs.
Bayesian methods tend to be model-specific and not easily generalized to modern ML models.
Bayesian methods do not provide guarantees on the frequentist coverage of their credible intervals.
Conformal prediction is a frequentist alternative that can be used for model-agnostic predictive inference like CP-based procedures for ITEs.
CP-based procedures can be used for inference of ITEs in a model-agnostic and distribution-free manner.
The fundamental problem of causal inference is related to never observing counter-factual outcomes in the context of ITEs.
The ITE is a difference between potential outcomes for an individual subject in a causal inference context.
Outcomes for an individual subject are the focus of the context, determining the effects of treatments on individuals.
The observed outcomes in the context are always factual, emphasizing the need to distinguish between treated and untreated outcomes.
The treatments are assigned to individual subjects, affecting the distributions of covariates in treated and untreated groups.
Covariate shift leads to differences in the distributions of covariates between treated and untreated groups, impacting the training data and target population distributions.
Unlike supervised learning that fits a single function using examples, models of treatment effects involve estimating nuisance parameters and indirect treatment effects.
ML-based CATE estimation addresses covariate shift, which can affect the generalization performance of ML models.
ML-based CATE estimation focuses on incorporating inductive biases to improve the accuracy of treatment effect estimates.
Inductive biases play a significant role in model-agnostic approaches for incorporating prior knowledge and assumptions into the predictive inference process.
The choice of the meta-learner has an influence on the estimation rates of CATE, as shown in references [5, 20].
The impact of CATE estimators on the generalization performance has been extensively studied to understand their effectiveness in different scenarios.
The validity and efficiency of predictive inference methods for ITE can be affected by the choice and performance of CATE estimators.
The proposed CP procedure aims to improve the predictive inference of ITEs by jointly addressing various factors and considerations in a systematic manner.
Meta-learners based on pseudo-outcome regression estimate transformed targets to provide insights into the relationship between observable variables and outcomes.
Conformal meta-learners focus on the distribution of covariates associated with pseudo-outcomes, ensuring consistency between training and testing data for reliable predictive inference.
